title: Counting Principles
blurb: The multiplication and addition principles, complementary counting, and organized lists — how to count without counting.
buildsOn: Nothing — the course starts here, from zero.

lessons.title: Organized Lists & Tree Diagrams
lessons.concreteComparison: A sandwich shop offers $2$ breads and $3$ fillings, and you try to name every possible sandwich out loud: "white-turkey, rye-cheese, um... did I say white-cheese?" Your friend grabs a napkin and writes them in a grid — white with each filling, then rye with each filling. Six sandwiches, none missed, none repeated. Counting starts with ORGANIZING.
lessons.objective: Count outcomes by building organized lists and tree diagrams, and read the total from the structure instead of tallying one by one.
concept: An **organized list** fixes the first part and cycles through the last: WT, WC, WV, then RT, RC, RV. The pattern is what makes it trustworthy — a random-order list can skip an outcome or repeat one, and you'd never know.

A **tree diagram** draws one level per decision: first a branch for each bread, then from EACH bread a branch for each filling. Every complete path from root to leaf is one outcome, so counting outcomes means counting leaves.

The tree exposes the shortcut: $2$ branches, each sprouting $3$ more, gives $2 \times 3 = 6$ leaves. You can know the count WITHOUT drawing — that idea becomes the multiplication principle in the next lesson.
Lists and trees stay useful forever: when a clever formula feels slippery, a small organized list is the ground truth you check it against.
lessons.keyIdea: Fix the first choice, cycle the last — an organized list can't miss and can't repeat, and a tree's leaves count themselves.

facts.title: Leaves of a tree
facts.explanation: A tree with b1 branches at level 1, b2 at level 2, and so on ends in the product of the branch counts.

facts.title: The list discipline
facts.explanation: Ordering the list like a dictionary orders words guarantees complete coverage with no duplicates.

workedExamples.statement: A sandwich shop offers $2$ breads (white, rye) and $3$ fillings (turkey, cheese, veggie). List every possible sandwich and count them.
workedExamples.solution: Fix the bread, cycle the filling. White: WT, WC, WV. Rye: RT, RC, RV. That is $3 + 3 = 6$ sandwiches — and the tree view says the same thing faster: $2 \times 3 = 6$.

workedExamples.statement: You flip a coin and then roll a standard die. Draw the tree in your head: how many outcomes are there in total?
workedExamples.solution: Level 1: $2$ branches (H, T). Level 2: each branch sprouts $6$ more (1 through 6). Leaves: $2 \times 6 = 12$ outcomes — H1 through H6, then T1 through T6.

workedExamples.statement: How many two-digit numbers can you build from the digits $1, 2, 3$ if no digit repeats? List them all.
workedExamples.solution: Fix the tens digit, cycle the units digit: 12, 13, then 21, 23, then 31, 32. Six numbers. Structure check: $3$ choices for the tens digit, then $2$ remaining for the units — $3 \times 2 = 6$. ✓

commonMistakes.text: Listing outcomes in whatever order they come to mind.
commonMistakes.correction: Unordered lists skip and repeat silently. Always fix the first part and cycle the last — if your list isn't in dictionary order, sort it and look for gaps.

commonMistakes.text: Counting tree BRANCHES instead of tree LEAVES.
commonMistakes.correction: Only complete root-to-leaf paths are outcomes. A coin-then-die tree has 8 branch segments at level 1 and 2 but exactly 12 leaves — the leaves are the answer.

tryIt.statement: Two coins are flipped. List the outcomes in an organized way and count them.
tryIt.solution: Fix the first coin: HH, HT, then TH, TT. Four outcomes — $2 \times 2 = 4$.

tryIt.statement: A pizza order is one size ($3$ options) and one crust ($2$ options). How many different pizzas are possible?
tryIt.solution: Tree: $3$ size branches, each with $2$ crust branches — $3 \times 2 = 6$ pizzas.

steps.title: Counting out loud fails
beats: Name every sandwich: $2$ breads, $3$ fillings. Out loud, you WILL lose track.
"White-turkey, rye-cheese... did I already say white-cheese?"
The fix isn't memory. It's ORGANIZATION.
This whole course starts with counting things without missing any.

steps.title: The organized list
beats: Fix the first part, cycle the last: WT, WC, WV — then RT, RC, RV.
Dictionary order: if your list were words, they'd be alphabetized.
Can't skip (the pattern has no holes). Can't repeat (the pattern never returns).
Six sandwiches, guaranteed.

steps.title: Spot the safe list
steps.prompt: Which list of two-coin outcomes is ORGANIZED (fix first coin, cycle second)?
steps.explanation: HH, HT, TH, TT fixes the first coin (H, then T) and cycles the second. The second option is scrambled — easy to lose your place. The third MISSED an outcome (TH) — exactly the failure organized lists prevent.

steps.title: The tree diagram
beats: One LEVEL per decision: first the bread splits, then each bread sprouts fillings.
Every root-to-leaf path is one complete outcome.
Counting outcomes $=$ counting leaves.
$2$ branches $\times$ $3$ sprouts each $= 6$ leaves — the tree counts itself.

steps.title: The outfit tree, live
steps.teach: Three shirts, two pairs of pants. Every complete path from the trunk to a leaf is one outfit — tap any leaf and watch its path light up. The tree doesn't just count the outcomes, it **lists** them, which is why it's the tool you reach for when the count is small enough to see.

steps.title: Leaves, not branches
steps.prompt: A spinner (A, B, C) is spun, then a coin is flipped. How many outcomes does the tree end in?
steps.explanation: $3$ branches, each sprouting $2$: $3 \times 2 = 6$ leaves. $5$ comes from ADDING $3 + 2$ — but the choices happen in sequence, not instead of each other. $9$ doubles up the spinner.

steps.title: The list is the ground truth
steps.body: Every formula in this course is a shortcut for a list you could write. When a formula feels slippery, shrink the problem until the list is small, write it out, and check the formula against it. Professionals do exactly this.

steps.title: Trees older than probability
steps.body: Tree diagrams appear in 13th-century writings of Ramon Llull, who used them to enumerate combinations of ideas — 400 years before Pascal and Fermat founded probability theory. The urge to count without missing anything is much older than the mathematics of chance it eventually powered.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Organized lists fix the first part and cycle the last — no skips, no repeats.
Tree diagrams grow one level per decision; outcomes are the LEAVES.
Branch counts multiply down the tree — the preview of the next lesson.

lessons.title: The Multiplication Principle
lessons.concreteComparison: Your closet holds $4$ shirts, $3$ pants, and $2$ pairs of shoes. Getting dressed is three decisions in a row — and the tree for it would have $4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24$ leaves. Nobody draws a 24-leaf tree. The multiplication principle IS that tree, compressed into one line of arithmetic.
lessons.objective: Count multi-stage choices with the multiplication principle, using the slot method for codes, plates, and passwords.
concept: **The multiplication principle:** if a task happens in stages — first a choice with $a$ options, THEN a choice with $b$ options, THEN one with $c$ — the total number of outcomes is $a \times b \times c$. It is exactly the leaf count of the tree you didn't draw.
The **slot method** makes it mechanical: draw one slot per decision, write the number of options in each slot, multiply. Outfits: $\underline{4} \times \underline{3} \times \underline{2} = 24$.

The one requirement: the NUMBER of options at each stage must not depend on which earlier option you picked. Each bread offers the same $3$ fillings; each first digit leaves the same $9$ remaining digits. The options themselves may change — only the count must hold steady.
"No repeats" problems fit perfectly: each slot's count shrinks by one. A $4$-digit PIN with all digits different: $10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7 = 5040$.
lessons.keyIdea: Stages in sequence multiply: one slot per decision, the option count in each slot, multiply across.

facts.title: The multiplication principle
facts.explanation: A stage with a options followed by stages with b, c, ... options produces the product — the leaf count of the corresponding tree.

facts.title: Shrinking slots
facts.explanation: When repeats are forbidden, each successive slot has one fewer option than the last.

workedExamples.statement: You own $4$ shirts, $3$ pants, and $2$ pairs of shoes. How many different outfits (one of each) can you wear?
workedExamples.solution: Three slots, one per decision: $\underline{4} \times \underline{3} \times \underline{2}$. Shirt first ($4$ ways), then pants ($3$ ways regardless of shirt), then shoes ($2$ ways). Total: $4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24$ outfits.

workedExamples.statement: A license plate has $2$ letters followed by $3$ digits, repeats allowed. How many plates are possible?
workedExamples.solution: Five slots: $\underline{26} \times \underline{26} \times \underline{10} \times \underline{10} \times \underline{10}$. Letters repeat, so both letter slots hold $26$; digits repeat, so each digit slot holds $10$. Total: $26^2 \times 10^3 = 676 \times 1000 = 676{,}000$.

workedExamples.statement: How many $4$-digit PIN codes use four DIFFERENT digits (0–9)?
workedExamples.solution: No-repeat slots shrink: first digit $10$ options, second $9$ (one is used up), third $8$, fourth $7$. Total: $10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7 = 5040$. Note which digits remain depends on your picks — but the COUNT at each slot is always the same, which is all the principle needs.

commonMistakes.text: Adding stage counts instead of multiplying.
commonMistakes.correction: "THEN" multiplies; only "OR" adds (next lesson). Four shirts then three pants is 4 × 3 = 12 combinations, not 7 — the 7 counts garments, not outfits.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting slots shrink when repeats are banned.
commonMistakes.correction: A 4-digit PIN with all digits different is 10·9·8·7, not 10·10·10·10. Ask at every slot: "how many options are LEFT?"

tryIt.statement: A cafe menu has $3$ mains, $4$ sides, and $2$ drinks. How many different meals (one of each)?
How many $3$-letter codes can be made from A–Z if letters may NOT repeat?

steps.title: The 24-leaf tree nobody draws
beats: $4$ shirts, $3$ pants, $2$ shoes: getting dressed is a tree.
$4$ branches, each sprouting $3$, each sprouting $2$ — $24$ leaves.
Drawing it: two minutes. Multiplying $4 \times 3 \times 2$: two seconds.
The multiplication principle IS the tree, compressed.

steps.title: Slots
beats: One slot per decision: $\underline{\;\;} \times \underline{\;\;} \times \underline{\;\;}$.
Write the OPTION COUNT in each slot.
Multiply across. Done.
Outfits: $\underline{4} \times \underline{3} \times \underline{2} = 24$.

steps.title: Menu math
steps.prompt: A menu has $3$ mains and $4$ drinks. How many main-plus-drink combos?
steps.explanation: Two slots: $3 \times 4 = 12$. The distractor $7$ is $3 + 4$ — adding counts the ITEMS, not the combos. "Then" multiplies.

steps.title: When slots are legal
beats: The COUNT in each slot must not depend on earlier picks.
PIN with no repeats: whichever first digit you chose, exactly $9$ remain.
The options DIFFER by path; the count doesn't. That's enough.
If a choice changes how MANY options come next — split into cases (next lesson).

steps.title: Three stages, one product
steps.teach: A drink, a main, a dessert. Each stage multiplies the branch count: $2 \times 3 \times 2 = 12$ complete meals. Notice you never had to list all twelve to know there are twelve — the tree's shape does the arithmetic. That's the multiplication principle: **stages multiply**.

steps.title: Repeats or not?
steps.prompt: How many $2$-letter codes from A–Z if the two letters must be DIFFERENT?
steps.explanation: The second slot lost one option: $26 \times 25 = 650$. $676$ allows repeats like AA; $51$ adds when it should multiply.

steps.title: Say the slots out loud
steps.body: Before multiplying, narrate: "first decision — how many ways? second decision — how many ways NOW?" The word "now" catches every shrinking-slot and every hidden restriction before it becomes a wrong answer.

steps.title: Why passwords want length
steps.body: Each extra character multiplies the count of possible passwords by the whole alphabet again. Eight lowercase letters give $26^8 \approx 209$ billion options; adding just TWO more characters multiplies that by $676$. Security experts preach length over cleverness because the multiplication principle does the heavy lifting.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Sequential stages multiply: one slot per decision, counts in the slots.
No-repeat problems shrink each successive slot by one.
Legal whenever the COUNT per slot is path-independent — else, case-split.

lessons.title: The Addition Principle & Case Work
lessons.concreteComparison: To get to school you can take one of $3$ buses OR one of $2$ trains. You make ONE trip — so you have $3 + 2 = 5$ ways, not $3 \times 2 = 6$. "Then" multiplied; "or" adds. Getting those two words straight is half of combinatorics.
lessons.objective: Add counts for exclusive alternatives, split hard problems into non-overlapping cases, and combine addition with multiplication in one problem.
concept: **The addition principle:** if a task is done in exactly ONE of several mutually exclusive ways — $a$ ways of the first kind OR $b$ ways of the second — the total is $a + b$. The alternatives must not overlap and must cover everything.
**Case work** is the addition principle used deliberately: split a hard count by some feature (first digit odd vs even, ends in 0 vs doesn't), count each case with slots, then ADD the cases.
Real problems mix the principles: EACH case is often a multiplication. "Cinema ($3$ movies $\times$ $4$ showtimes) or theater ($2$ plays $\times$ $3$ showtimes)" $= 12 + 6 = 18$ evenings.

The two danger checks before adding: do my cases OVERLAP (something counted twice)? Do they COVER everything (something counted never)? A good case split is a partition — every outcome in exactly one case.
lessons.keyIdea: "Or" adds, "then" multiplies — split into non-overlapping cases, multiply within each, add across.

facts.title: The addition principle
facts.explanation: One trip, several kinds of route: add the counts, provided no route is counted in two kinds.

facts.title: Mixing the principles
facts.explanation: Multiply inside each case, add across cases — the standard shape of a real counting problem.

workedExamples.statement: A town offers $3$ bus routes and $2$ train lines to the city. You take exactly one. How many ways can you travel?
workedExamples.solution: The trip is a bus OR a train — never both. Exclusive alternatives add: $3 + 2 = 5$ ways.

workedExamples.statement: Friday options: the cinema shows $3$ movies at $4$ times each; the theater shows $2$ plays at $3$ times each. How many different evenings out are possible?
workedExamples.solution: Case 1 (cinema): movie THEN time $= 3 \times 4 = 12$. Case 2 (theater): play THEN time $= 2 \times 3 = 6$. The cases can't overlap (you're in one building), so add: $12 + 6 = 18$ evenings.

workedExamples.statement: How many two-digit numbers have BOTH digits odd or BOTH digits even? (A two-digit number can't start with $0$.)
workedExamples.solution: Case 1 — both odd: tens from $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$ ($5$ ways), units from the same set ($5$ ways): $5 \times 5 = 25$. Case 2 — both even: tens from $\{2,4,6,8\}$ ($4$ ways — no leading zero), units from $\{0,2,4,6,8\}$ ($5$ ways): $4 \times 5 = 20$. No number is in both cases (digits can't be odd AND even), so add: $25 + 20 = 45$.

commonMistakes.correction: 3 buses or 2 trains is 5 ways, not 6. Multiplying says you ride a bus AND THEN a train on the same trip. Ask: do both things happen, or exactly one?
Counting numbers "divisible by 2" and "divisible by 3" as separate cases double-counts 6, 12, 18... Cases must partition: every outcome in exactly ONE case. (Overlaps get their own tool in Lesson 6.)

tryIt.statement: Lunch is one starter: $3$ soups or $4$ salads. How many starter choices do you have?
tryIt.solution: Exactly one starter, soup OR salad: $3 + 4 = 7$.

tryIt.statement: A shop sells bikes in $4$ colors with $2$ frame sizes, and scooters in $3$ colors (one size). How many different vehicles could you buy?
tryIt.solution: Bikes: $4 \times 2 = 8$. Scooters: $3$. One vehicle, so add: $8 + 3 = 11$.

steps.title: Bus or train?
beats: $3$ buses OR $2$ trains — you make ONE trip.
Multiplying would mean bus AND THEN train, same morning.
"Then" multiplies. "Or" adds. Half of combinatorics is hearing which one.

steps.title: When branches don't match, you add
steps.teach: Order pizza and you pick one of 3 sizes; order pasta and you pick one of 2 shapes. The tree is **ragged** — the cases have different follow-ups — so there's no clean product. Count each case and add: $3 + 2 = 5$ possible orders. Multiplication is for stages; addition is for cases.

steps.title: Or vs then
steps.prompt: A vending machine has $5$ chip flavors and $4$ candy bars. You buy ONE snack. How many choices?
steps.explanation: One snack — chips OR candy: $5 + 4 = 9$. The distractor $20$ multiplies, which would mean buying chips AND THEN candy.

steps.title: Case work
beats: Hard counts split along a feature: both digits odd vs both even.
Count each case with SLOTS (multiplication inside).
ADD across cases.
Two checks before adding: no overlap, nothing missed. A perfect split is a partition.

steps.title: Is this split safe?
steps.prompt: To count two-digit numbers, Bilguun splits into cases "contains a 7" and "is even". What's wrong?
options: The cases overlap — 70 is in both
steps.explanation: $70$ contains a $7$ AND is even — it would be counted twice. Cases must be mutually exclusive before you add. (Lesson 6 shows how to FIX overlapping counts by subtracting the overlap.)

steps.title: Underline the or
steps.body: Read the problem and literally underline every "or", "either", "one of" (add signals) and every "then", "and", "followed by", "each" (multiply signals). The arithmetic almost writes itself once the words are sorted.

steps.title: The menu-combinatorics industry
steps.body: A fast-food chain once advertised "over 250 ways to order a burger" — a case-work count over toppings done by an ad agency. Combinatorial menu claims got so popular that a competitor countered with a number in the hundreds of thousands, computed with the same two principles you just learned. Marketing departments run on "or adds, then multiplies".

steps.title: What you learned
points: Exclusive alternatives ADD; sequential stages MULTIPLY.
Case work: split by a feature, multiply inside cases, add across.
Before adding, check the split: no overlaps, full coverage.

lessons.title: Counting with Restrictions
lessons.concreteComparison: Four friends pile into a row of $4$ cinema seats, but Ana HAS to have the aisle seat — she always spills the popcorn. Suddenly the count isn't $4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1$ anymore. Restrictions don't break the slot method; they just tell you which slot to fill FIRST.
lessons.objective: Count arrangements under restrictions by seating the fussiest requirement first, and count must-sit-together problems with the block trick.
concept: **Fussy first:** when a slot or a person is restricted, handle it before anything else. Ana takes the aisle ($1$ way), THEN the other three fill the remaining seats: $1 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6$. Filling unrestricted slots first creates contradictions and double-counts.

**The block trick** for "must sit together": glue the pair into one super-person. Arrange the (now fewer) units, then multiply by the arrangements INSIDE the block. Four people with A and B adjacent: $3$ units arrange in $3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6$ ways, times $2$ internal orders $= 12$.
**"Must NOT sit together"** is usually easiest indirectly: all arrangements minus the together ones. (That subtraction habit becomes a whole lesson next.)
Restrictions on PROPERTIES work the same way: to count $3$-digit even numbers, fill the units digit first (it's the restricted one), then the free slots.
lessons.keyIdea: Seat the fussiest first; glue must-be-together items into a block and multiply by the block's internal arrangements.

facts.title: Fussy first
facts.explanation: Fill the restricted slot before the free ones — the free slots then shrink normally.

facts.title: The block trick
facts.explanation: Glue the together-pair into one unit, arrange the units, then multiply by the orders within the glue.

workedExamples.statement: Five books go on a shelf. The dictionary must be on the far left. How many arrangements are there?
workedExamples.solution: Fussy first: the dictionary's slot is forced ($1$ way). The remaining $4$ books fill $4$ slots freely: $4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 24$. Total: $1 \times 24 = 24$ arrangements.

workedExamples.statement: Four friends sit in a row, and Ana and Bat insist on sitting next to each other. How many seatings are possible?
workedExamples.solution: Glue Ana+Bat into one block. Now $3$ units (block, C, D) arrange in $3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6$ ways. Inside the block, Ana and Bat can order themselves $2$ ways (AB or BA). Total: $6 \times 2 = 12$ seatings.

workedExamples.statement: How many $3$-digit EVEN numbers use the digits $1$–$5$ with no digit repeated?
workedExamples.solution: The restriction lives in the units digit: it must be even, so it comes from $\{2, 4\}$ — $2$ ways. Fussy first! Then the hundreds digit: $4$ digits remain, all legal — $4$ ways. Then the tens digit: $3$ remain. Total: $2 \times 4 \times 3 = 24$ numbers.

commonMistakes.text: Filling the easy slots first and hitting a contradiction.
commonMistakes.correction: For 3-digit even numbers from 1–5 no repeat: choosing the first two digits first sometimes uses up both 2 AND 4, leaving nothing legal for the units slot. The slot counts stop being path-independent — which is exactly why the restricted slot goes first.
Gluing Ana and Bat and arranging 3 units gives 6 — but Ana-Bat and Bat-Ana are different seatings. Multiply by the 2 inside orders: 12.

tryIt.statement: Four runners finish a race with no ties. Dulguun definitely finishes first. How many finish orders are possible?
tryIt.solution: Dulguun's slot is forced; the other three arrange freely: $3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6$.

tryIt.statement: Five friends line up for a photo; the twins insist on standing together. How many lineups?
tryIt.solution: Glue the twins: $4$ units arrange in $4! = 24$ ways, times $2$ inside orders $= 48$ lineups.

steps.title: Ana wants the aisle
beats: Four friends, four seats — but Ana MUST take the aisle.
Her seat isn't a choice anymore. It's forced: $1$ way.
Then the free people fill free seats: $3 \times 2 \times 1$.
Restrictions don't break slots — they set the ORDER you fill them.

steps.title: Fussy first
beats: Always fill the restricted slot (or seat the restricted person) FIRST.
Restricted-last risks contradictions: the legal options may be used up.
Even numbers? Units digit first. VIP on the end? VIP first.
After the fussy slot, everything shrinks normally.

steps.title: Which slot first?
steps.prompt: Counting $4$-letter codes from A–F, no repeats, that END in a vowel (A or E). Which slot do you fill first?
steps.explanation: The restriction lives in the LAST slot, so it goes first: $2$ ways, then $5 \times 4 \times 3$ for the free slots. Filling free slots first can consume both vowels, and the slot counts stop being reliable.

steps.title: The no-repeats tree
steps.teach: Build a 3-digit code from the digits 1, 2, 3 with no digit repeated. Watch the branches **shrink**: 3 choices, then 2, then 1 — the restriction eats one option at every stage. $3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6$ codes. Tap a leaf to walk one.

steps.title: The block trick
beats: "Must sit TOGETHER" → glue them into one super-person.
Arrange the units (there's one fewer now).
Multiply by the arrangements INSIDE the glue.
Four people, two glued: $3! \times 2! = 12$.

steps.title: Don't forget the inside
steps.prompt: Six students line up; two best friends must be adjacent. How many lineups?
steps.explanation: Glue the friends: $5$ units arrange in $5! = 120$ ways, times $2$ orders inside the block $= 240$. Forgetting the inside gives $120$; $720$ ignores the restriction entirely.

steps.title: "NOT together" wants subtraction
steps.body: "Must not sit together" is easiest as ALL arrangements minus TOGETHER arrangements: for the six students, $720 - 240 = 480$. Counting the thing you don't want and subtracting is the next lesson's whole superpower.

steps.title: Wedding planners are combinatorialists
steps.body: Professional seating-chart software works exactly like this lesson: hard constraints (the feuding uncles must NOT be adjacent, grandma must face the stage) get placed first, then the free guests fill remaining seats. For 150 guests the unrestricted count has more arrangements than atoms in the observable universe — restrictions are the only reason charts are possible at all.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Fill restricted slots first; free slots shrink normally after.
Together-pairs: glue, arrange units, multiply by inside orders.
Not-together: all minus together — subtraction, coming next.

lessons.title: Complementary Counting
lessons.concreteComparison: "How many $3$-child families have AT LEAST ONE girl?" You could count families with exactly one girl, then exactly two, then exactly three, and add... or you could count the ONE family type with NO girls and subtract it from all $8$: $8 - 1 = 7$. When the thing you want has many shapes and the thing you don't want has one, count the enemy.
lessons.objective: Count "at least one" and other sprawling events by counting the complement and subtracting from the total.
concept: **Complementary counting:** $\text{want} = \text{total} - \text{don't want}$. It's legal because every outcome is in exactly one of the two piles — the piles partition the total.

The trigger phrase is **"at least one"**: its complement is the single clean case **"none"**. At least one girl ↔ no girls. At least one repeated digit ↔ all digits different. At least one six ↔ no sixes.

The method needs TWO counts you can actually do: the total (usually a clean multiplication) and the complement (usually another clean multiplication). If both are easy and the direct count is case soup, complement wins.
The one discipline: the complement must be EXACTLY everything you don't want — no more, no less. Write it in words before computing: "the opposite of at-least-one-repeat is ALL-DIFFERENT".
lessons.keyIdea: Want = total − don't want; "at least one X" is the complement of "no X at all".

facts.title: The complement rule
facts.explanation: Every outcome falls in exactly one pile, so the two piles' counts add to the total.

facts.title: The trigger
facts.explanation: "At least one" sprawls over many cases; "none" is a single multiplication.

workedExamples.statement: Three coins are flipped. How many outcomes contain at least one head?
workedExamples.solution: Total outcomes: $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$. The complement of "at least one head" is "NO heads" — only TTT, which is $1$ outcome. Want: $8 - 1 = 7$.

workedExamples.statement: How many $4$-digit PIN codes (digits 0–9, repeats allowed) have at least one repeated digit?
workedExamples.solution: Total PINs: $10^4 = 10{,}000$. Complement — ALL digits different: $10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7 = 5040$. At least one repeat: $10000 - 5040 = 4960$. Counting repeats directly would need cases for doubles, triples, two-pairs, quads — the complement does it in one subtraction.

workedExamples.statement: Two dice are rolled. How many outcomes show at least one $6$?
workedExamples.solution: Total: $6 \times 6 = 36$. Complement — NO sixes: each die has $5$ legal faces, $5 \times 5 = 25$. At least one six: $36 - 25 = 11$. (Direct check: six pairs $(6, x)$, six pairs $(x, 6)$, minus the double-counted $(6,6)$: $6 + 6 - 1 = 11$ ✓.)

commonMistakes.text: Taking the complement of only part of the condition.
commonMistakes.correction: The opposite of "at least one head in three flips" is "no heads", not "at least one tail". Write the complement in words and check: together the two piles must cover EVERYTHING exactly once.
For 4-digit PINs the total is 10^4 = 10,000 — not 9·10^3 (PINs may start with 0, unlike 4-digit NUMBERS). Nail down the universe before subtracting from it.

tryIt.statement: Five coins are flipped. How many outcomes have at least one tail?
tryIt.solution: Total $2^5 = 32$; complement (no tails — all heads) is $1$. Answer: $32 - 1 = 31$.

tryIt.statement: How many $3$-letter codes from A–Z (repeats allowed) contain at least one letter A?
tryIt.solution: Total $26^3 = 17576$; complement — no A anywhere: $25^3 = 15625$. Answer: $17576 - 15625 = 1951$.

steps.title: Count the enemy
beats: "At least one girl" in 3 kids: exactly 1, exactly 2, exactly 3... three cases. Ugh.
"NO girls": one case. BBB. Count: $1$.
All families: $2^3 = 8$. Subtract: $8 - 1 = 7$.
When what you want sprawls and what you don't want is tiny — count the enemy.

steps.title: Total minus opposite
beats: $\text{want} = \text{total} - \text{don't want}$.
Legal because every outcome is in EXACTLY one pile.
Both counts must be doable: total (easy multiplication), complement (easy multiplication).
Trigger phrase: "at least one" — its opposite is the single case "none".

steps.title: What's the complement?
steps.prompt: You want to count $5$-flip sequences with at least one head. What exactly is the complement?
steps.explanation: The opposite of "at least one head" is "NO heads" — all tails, a single outcome. "At least one tail" overlaps with the want-pile (HTTTT has both); "exactly zero tails" means all heads, which is IN the want pile.

steps.title: Subtract from WHAT?
steps.prompt: Counting $4$-digit PINs (0000–9999) with at least one repeated digit — what total do you subtract from?
steps.explanation: PINs may start with 0, so the universe is $10^4$. The $9000$ counts 4-digit NUMBERS (1000–9999) — a different universe. And $5040$ is the complement itself, not the total.

steps.title: Write the opposite in words first
steps.body: Before any arithmetic, finish this sentence: "the opposite of ___ is exactly ___". If you can't state the complement cleanly in words, the subtraction will be wrong no matter how careful the multiplication is.

steps.title: The birthday problem runs on this
steps.body: The famous fact that just $23$ people give a better-than-even chance of a shared birthday is a complement count: computing "at least two share" directly is a nightmare, but "all $23$ birthdays different" is one long multiplication. Subtract from 1 and the bar bet is won. You'll meet it properly in Unit 5.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Want = total − don't want; the piles must partition the universe.
"At least one X" flips to the single clean case "no X".
State the complement in words, and nail the correct total, before subtracting.

lessons.title: Overcounting & Inclusion-Exclusion
lessons.concreteComparison: Your class survey says $18$ students play soccer and $12$ play basketball, so the coach announces "$30$ athletes!" — but the room only has $25$ students. The $7$ kids who play BOTH got counted twice, once per list. Real count: $18 + 12 - 7 = 23$. When lists overlap, adding them overcounts — and the fix is to subtract the overlap right back out.
lessons.objective: Correct overlapping counts with the two-set inclusion-exclusion formula, and divide out symmetric double-counting.
concept: Adding overlapping groups counts the overlap twice. The repair is **inclusion-exclusion**: $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$ — include both lists, exclude the double-counted intersection once.
A **Venn diagram** shows why: the middle region sits inside both circles, so $|A| + |B|$ covers it twice. Subtracting $|A \cap B|$ once leaves every region counted exactly once.
The same idea counts numbers with divisibility: multiples of $2$ or $5$ up to $100$ are $50 + 20 - 10 = 60$, because multiples of $10$ live on both lists.

A second flavor of overcounting is SYMMETRIC: counting every handshake from each shaker's side counts each handshake exactly twice — so divide by $2$. Six people: $\frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15$ handshakes. "Count with order, divide by the repeats" is the seed of combinations (Unit 3).
lessons.keyIdea: |A or B| = |A| + |B| − |A and B|; and when every outcome is counted k times by symmetry, divide by k.

facts.title: Inclusion-exclusion (two sets)
facts.explanation: Add both sets, then subtract the intersection once — it was included twice.

facts.title: Symmetric overcount
facts.explanation: Ordered counting sees each handshake twice (A-with-B and B-with-A); divide by the repetition factor.

workedExamples.statement: In a class, $18$ students play soccer, $12$ play basketball, and $7$ play both. How many students play at least one of the two sports?
workedExamples.solution: $|S \cup B| = |S| + |B| - |S \cap B| = 18 + 12 - 7 = 23$. The $7$ two-sport players were on both lists; subtracting them once leaves everyone counted exactly once.

workedExamples.statement: How many integers from $1$ to $100$ are divisible by $2$ or by $5$?
workedExamples.solution: Multiples of $2$: $50$. Multiples of $5$: $20$. Both lists share the multiples of $10$: there are $10$ of them. Inclusion-exclusion: $50 + 20 - 10 = 60$.

workedExamples.statement: Six people at a meeting each shake hands with everyone else exactly once. How many handshakes happen?
workedExamples.solution: Count with order first: each of $6$ people shakes $5$ hands — $6 \times 5 = 30$ ordered shakes. But the count sees every handshake TWICE (Ana-with-Bat and Bat-with-Ana are the same shake). Divide by $2$: $\frac{30}{2} = 15$ handshakes.

commonMistakes.correction: 18 + 12 = 30 athletes in a 25-student class is the overlap shouting at you. Any time one item can be on BOTH lists, subtract the intersection: 18 + 12 − 7 = 23.
The intersection was included TWICE, so subtract it ONCE — it should end up counted once, not zero times. Students who play both sports are still athletes.

tryIt.statement: Of $40$ students, $22$ like math, $17$ like physics, and $9$ like both. How many like at least one subject?
tryIt.solution: $22 + 17 - 9 = 30$ students like at least one. (And $40 - 30 = 10$ like neither — complement!)

tryIt.statement: Eight chess players all play each other once. How many games is that?
tryIt.solution: Ordered count: $8 \times 7 = 56$; each game counted twice, so $\frac{56}{2} = 28$ games.

steps.title: Thirty athletes, twenty-five students
beats: $18$ soccer $+ 12$ basketball $= 30$ athletes, says the coach.
The class has $25$ students. Something's broken.
The $7$ kids who play BOTH are on both lists — counted twice.
$18 + 12 - 7 = 23$. Overlaps must be subtracted back out.

steps.title: Include, include, exclude
beats: $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$.
Venn view: the middle region sits in both circles.
$|A| + |B|$ covers it twice; subtracting the intersection once fixes it.
Every region counted exactly once — that's always the goal.

steps.title: The double-count, caught in the act
steps.teach: A class where 20 play sports, 15 play music, and 6 do both. Press **next** and watch the naive sum $20 + 15$ count those 6 students twice — then watch the subtraction put it right. That correction IS inclusion–exclusion: $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$.

steps.title: Music survey
steps.prompt: $25$ students like pop, $15$ like rock, $8$ like both. How many like at least one?
steps.explanation: $25 + 15 - 8 = 32$. The distractor $40$ forgets the overlap; $17$ subtracts $8$ from just one list instead of from the sum.

steps.title: Symmetric overcounting
beats: Six people all shake hands. Count from each side: $6 \times 5 = 30$.
Every handshake got counted TWICE — once per shaker.
Divide by the repeat factor: $30 / 2 = 15$.
"Count ordered, divide by repeats" — remember this; it becomes combinations in Unit 3.

steps.title: Tournament games
steps.prompt: Ten teams each play every other team once. How many games?
steps.explanation: Ordered: $10 \times 9 = 90$, but each game was counted from both teams' sides — divide by $2$: $45$. The $100$ is $10^2$, which would even let teams play themselves.

steps.title: Overcount alarms
steps.body: Two alarms worth installing: (1) if a combined count exceeds the room, an overlap got double-counted; (2) if every outcome was reachable in several equivalent orders, you counted it that many times — divide. Ask of every count: "how many times did I see each outcome?" The answer should be exactly one.

steps.title: The formula that audits databases
steps.body: Inclusion-exclusion is how analysts de-duplicate mailing lists and how epidemiologists estimate how many patients appear in multiple hospital registries. The three-set version adds the pairwise overlaps back differently — and the general formula alternates + and − forever, one of the prettiest patterns in combinatorics. You'll extend it in the units ahead.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Overlapping lists: add both, subtract the intersection once.
Symmetric overcounts: count ordered, divide by the repeat factor.
Audit every count: each outcome seen exactly once.

practice.statement: A T-shirt comes in $5$ colors and $3$ sizes. How many different T-shirts can the shop stock?
practice.solution: Two slots: color THEN size — $5 \times 3 = 15$ different shirts.

practice.statement: There are $4$ roads from town A to town B, and $3$ roads from B to C. How many routes go from A to C through B?
practice.solution: Choose a first leg, THEN a second leg: $4 \times 3 = 12$ routes.

practice.statement: A lunch special is one starter ($3$ soups OR $4$ salads) followed by one of $2$ mains. How many different lunches are possible?
practice.solution: Starter choices add (it's one starter): $3 + 4 = 7$. Then a main: $7 \times 2 = 14$ lunches.

practice.statement: How many $3$-digit codes use digits $0$–$9$ with no repeated digit, if the first digit may not be $0$?
practice.solution: Fussy first: the first slot avoids $0$ — $9$ ways. Second slot: any unused digit, including $0$ — $9$ ways. Third slot: $8$ remain. Total: $9 \times 9 \times 8 = 648$.

practice.statement: Four friends sit in a row and Bilguun insists on an END seat. How many seatings are possible?
practice.solution: Seat Bilguun first: $2$ end seats. The other three fill the rest: $3! = 6$. Total: $2 \times 6 = 12$.

practice.statement: Five coins are flipped. How many outcomes contain at least one head?
practice.solution: Total $2^5 = 32$; complement (no heads — all tails) is $1$ outcome. At least one head: $32 - 1 = 31$.

practice.statement: How many integers from $1$ to $50$ are divisible by $3$ or by $7$?
practice.solution: Multiples of $3$: $16$ (up to $48$). Multiples of $7$: $7$ (up to $49$). Both — multiples of $21$: $2$ ($21, 42$). Inclusion-exclusion: $16 + 7 - 2 = 21$.

practice.statement: Two dice are rolled. How many outcomes show at least one $5$?
practice.solution: Total $36$; complement — no fives: $5 \times 5 = 25$. At least one five: $36 - 25 = 11$.

testYourself.statement: A license plate is $3$ different letters followed by $2$ digits (digits may repeat). How many plates are possible?
testYourself.solution: Letters shrink: $26 \times 25 \times 24 = 15{,}600$. Digits repeat: $10 \times 10 = 100$. Total: $15600 \times 100 = 1{,}560{,}000$ plates.

testYourself.statement: How many $4$-digit numbers use the digits $1$–$9$, all different, and are EVEN?
testYourself.solution: Fussy first — the units digit must be even, from $\{2,4,6,8\}$: $4$ ways. Then the thousands digit: $8$ digits remain — $8$ ways; hundreds: $7$; tens: $6$. Total: $4 \times 8 \times 7 \times 6 = 1344$.

testYourself.statement: Five people sit in a row, but two of them refuse to sit next to each other. How many seatings are possible?
testYourself.solution: All seatings: $5! = 120$. Together (block trick): glue the pair — $4$ units arrange in $4! = 24$ ways, times $2$ inside orders $= 48$. Not together: $120 - 48 = 72$.

testYourself.statement: How many $3$-digit numbers ($100$–$999$) have at least one repeated digit?
testYourself.solution: Total $3$-digit numbers: $900$. Complement — all digits different: first digit $9$ (no zero), second $9$ (zero now allowed, one digit used), third $8$: $9 \times 9 \times 8 = 648$. At least one repeat: $900 - 648 = 252$.

testYourself.statement: Of $40$ students, $22$ like math, $17$ like physics, and $9$ like both. How many like NEITHER subject?
testYourself.solution: At least one: $22 + 17 - 9 = 30$ (inclusion-exclusion). Neither — complement: $40 - 30 = 10$ students.

testYourself.statement: Friday plans: the cinema has $3$ movies at $4$ times each, the theater has $2$ plays at $3$ times each, and the arcade is a single option. How many different plans are possible?
testYourself.solution: Three exclusive cases: cinema $3 \times 4 = 12$; theater $2 \times 3 = 6$; arcade $1$. One plan, so add: $12 + 6 + 1 = 19$ plans.
